# 10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026

## Learn about key insights on cabinet colors, layouts, budgets and more from the 2026 U.S. Houzz Kitchen Trends Study

[![Mitchell Parker](https://st.hzcdn.com/fimgs/fca3b09f00e89059_5619-w40-h40-b0-p0--.jpg)](https://www.houzz.com/user/mitchell-parker)

[Mitchell Parker](https://www.houzz.com/user/mitchell-parker) January 13, 2026

Houzz Editorial Staff. Home design journalist writing about cool spaces, innovative trends, breaking news, industry analysis and humor.

More

SaveComment4Like40

Share

TwitterEmail

Print

Kitchen remodels are revealing clear shifts in priorities for the home. Wood cabinets are dethroning white, and aging-in-place design is on the rise.Renovating homeowners are rethinking layouts, planning for the long term and investing in smarter, more functional spaces, often without making kitchens any bigger. Find out more about these and other key insights from the just-released 2026 U.S. Houzz Kitchen Trends Study.

[Cannonade Addition + Remodel](https://www.houzz.com/photos/cannonade-addition-remodel-transitional-kitchen-austin-phvw-vp~214845903 "Cannonade Addition + Remodel")[Urbane Design](https://www.houzz.com/user/urbanedesign) Save Photo

[10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026](https://www.houzz.com/hznb/photos/10-kitchen-remodeling-trends-to-know-for-2026-phvw-vp~215066133 "10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026") Save Photo

**1\. Wood Cabinets Overtake White**

Wood has claimed the top spot for kitchen cabinet color. Nearly 3 in 10 renovating homeowners (29%) are choosing wood cabinets — a 6-percentage-point jump from the previous year, pushing white into second place at 28% after a 5-point decline in that period. Medium wood tones lead the pack (15%), followed by light wood (11%) and dark wood (3%), signaling a shift toward warmth and natural character.

Off-white holds steady at 15%. Green (6%) now edges out gray (5%), while blue, beige, black and greige remain niche options, each used in fewer than 5% of renovated kitchens.

[Rocklin Home Remodel](https://www.houzz.com/photos/rocklin-home-remodel-transitional-kitchen-sacramento-phvw-vp~214703142 "Rocklin Home Remodel")[Usher Building & Design Inc.](https://www.houzz.com/user/usherbuilding) Save Photo

Warm midtone wood cabinets surround a rich, dark wood island in this inviting Sacramento, California, kitchen by [Usher Building & Design](https://www.houzz.com/pro/usherbuilding/usher-building-and-design-inc), which uses [Houzz Pro](https://www.houzz.com/hire-pro/why-hire-a-pro-using-houzz-pro?source=platform_ideabooks) software.

[See why you should hire a professional who uses Houzz Pro software](https://www.houzz.com/hire-pro/why-hire-a-pro-using-houzz-pro?source=platform_ideabooks)

[10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026](https://www.houzz.com/hznb/photos/10-kitchen-remodeling-trends-to-know-for-2026-phvw-vp~215066134 "10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026") Save Photo

**2\. Aging-in-Place Planning Moves to the Forefront**

Designing for aging and long-term needs is becoming a bigger priority in kitchen remodels. More than half of renovating homeowners (53%) address current or future special needs in their kitchen projects — up 3 percentage points from the previous year. These include updates for aging household members, pets, household members with disabilities and young children. While fewer renovating homeowners expect the special needs to arise within the next year (25%, down 4 points), most are planning ahead: 52% anticipate needs emerging in five or more years, with smaller shares looking one to two years out (9%) or three to four years out (14%).

Aging-related updates are driving the shift. Among renovating homeowners addressing current needs, nearly one-third (31%) focus on aging household members, up 5 points year over year. Planning for future aging needs is even more common, at 41% (up 6 points). By comparison, far fewer renovating homeowners design for pets (8% current; 5% future), household members with disabilities (6% current; 7% future) or young children (5% for both), with several of these categories declining year over year.

When homeowners do plan for aging, they overwhelmingly prioritize safety and ease of use. Nine in 10 (90%) include accessibility features, a 2-point increase from the previous year. As this graphic shows, pullout cabinets lead the list (59%), followed by additional lighting (51%) and wide drawer pulls (44%). Rounded countertops (34%) and nonslip flooring (32%) are also popular, while more specialized upgrades — such as wheelchair-accessible doorways (21%), lower fixtures (15%) and lower countertops (5%) — remain less common.

[10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026](https://www.houzz.com/hznb/photos/10-kitchen-remodeling-trends-to-know-for-2026-phvw-vp~215066110 "10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026") Save Photo

**3\. Layout Changes Lead the List of Major Upgrades**

Reworking the kitchen layout is the most common major upgrade. More than half of renovating homeowners (52%) modify their kitchen’s layout, outpacing upgrades to home systems (42%) and wall updates (33%), the latter of which is down 3 percentage points from the previous year.

Other big changes are far less common. Just 11% alter the ceiling, 5% move the kitchen to a new location (down slightly year over year) and 4% make structural changes to the flooring.

[10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026](https://www.houzz.com/hznb/photos/10-kitchen-remodeling-trends-to-know-for-2026-phvw-vp~215066111 "10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026") Save Photo

When homeowners do rethink the layout, familiar configurations still dominate. L-shaped kitchens lead at 35%, followed closely by U-shaped layouts at 31%. Galley kitchens account for 14% of kitchens with a changed layout.

[25 Home Design Trends Defining How We’ll Live in 2026](https://www.houzz.com/magazine/25-home-design-trends-defining-how-well-live-in-2026-stsetivw-vs~183837788)

[10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026](https://www.houzz.com/hznb/photos/10-kitchen-remodeling-trends-to-know-for-2026-phvw-vp~215066112 "10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026") Save Photo

**4\. Most Kitchens Keep the Same Footprint**

Even as homeowners rethink how their kitchens work, most aren’t making them bigger. Although 52% of renovating homeowners change the layout, more than two-thirds of kitchens (68%) end up about the same size after renovation — a share that’s up 4 percentage points from the previous year. That means many homeowners and pros are finding smarter ways to reconfigure kitchens within the existing footprint.

Expansion is the exception. About a quarter of kitchens (26%) grow somewhat, down 2 percentage points year over year, and just 6% become significantly larger. When kitchens do expand, they most often borrow space from adjacent rooms. Of the 32% of renovating homeowners who enlarge their kitchen footprint, 25% take space from dining rooms, followed by breakfast nooks (17%) and home additions (16%). Smaller shares expand into hallways (10%) or living rooms (8%), with even fewer tapping laundry rooms (7%), family rooms (6%) or closets (5%).

[10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026](https://www.houzz.com/hznb/photos/10-kitchen-remodeling-trends-to-know-for-2026-phvw-vp~215066113 "10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026") Save Photo

Looking at final size, half of renovated kitchens (50%) measure 200 square feet or more. Another 38% fall between 100 and 199 square feet, a share that’s up 4 points, while only 12% come in under 100 square feet, down 1 point.

[Classic Southern Home](https://www.houzz.com/photos/classic-southern-home-traditional-kitchen-atlanta-phvw-vp~214681955 "Classic Southern Home")[Kandrac & Kole Interior Designs, Inc.](https://www.houzz.com/user/kandrackole) Save Photo

This roomy, light and airy Atlanta kitchen by [Kandrac & Kole Interior Designs](https://www.houzz.com/hsc/aetrk/k=HBkcFQYVGhbiu_-) includes a walk-in pantry on the left and a separate butler’s pantry through the arched opening on the right.

[10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026](https://www.houzz.com/hznb/photos/10-kitchen-remodeling-trends-to-know-for-2026-phvw-vp~215066109 "10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026") Save Photo

**5\. Functionality Becomes a Stronger Remodeling Driver**

Practical concerns are increasingly pushing kitchen projects forward. More renovating homeowners now cite kitchen deterioration or dysfunction as a reason to remodel, with the share rising 3 percentage points year over year to 38%. That puts functionality nearly on par with dissatisfaction with the old kitchen’s style (41%).

Other motivations remain steady. About a third of renovating homeowners cite having the financial means to renovate (33%), while a quarter remodel to improve resale value (25%). Meanwhile, fewer homeowners are renovating to personalize a recently purchased home, with that share dropping 4 points to 24%.

Urgent fixes are still relatively rare. Just 4% of homeowners remodel to address damage from a disaster, and only 3% cite safety or health concerns as the primary trigger.

[Alpharetta kitchen project](https://www.houzz.com/photos/alpharetta-kitchen-project-transitional-kitchen-atlanta-phvw-vp~214625812 "Alpharetta kitchen project")[YB Interiors, LLC](https://www.houzz.com/user/ybinteriors) Save Photo

[YB Interiors](https://www.houzz.com/pro/ybinteriors/yb-interiors-llc), which uses Houzz Pro, transformed this Atlanta kitchen from [dark and dated](https://www.houzz.com/hznb/photos/img-6919-png-transitional-phvw-vp~212890076) to bright, functional and inviting.

[10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026](https://www.houzz.com/hznb/photos/10-kitchen-remodeling-trends-to-know-for-2026-phvw-vp~215066131 "10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026") Save Photo

**6\. Style Updates Dip Slightly, With Transitional Still on Top**

Style updates remain a major focus in kitchen remodels, even as they dip slightly year over year. A vast majority of renovating homeowners (78%) still change their kitchen’s style, despite a 3-percentage-point decline from the previous year. Among those making a style shift, transitional continues to lead at 25%, followed by traditional (12%), modern (11%) and contemporary (10%). Midcentury remains a niche choice, selected by just 6% of homeowners.

[Marion Seaside Colonial](https://www.houzz.com/photos/marion-seaside-colonial-transitional-kitchen-boston-phvw-vp~214799938 "Marion Seaside Colonial")[Beige and Bleu Design Studio](https://www.houzz.com/user/beigeandbleu) Save Photo

[Beige and Bleu Design Studio](https://www.houzz.com/pro/beigeandbleu/beige-and-bleu-design-studio) gave this Boston kitchen a transitional look, featuring sage green cabinets paired with warm wood accents.

[10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026](https://www.houzz.com/hznb/photos/10-kitchen-remodeling-trends-to-know-for-2026-phvw-vp~215066114 "10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026") Save Photo

**7\. Kitchen Budgets Swing Widely by Scope and Size**

Kitchen remodeling costs vary dramatically depending on project scope and kitchen size. We’ll start with major kitchen remodels, in which all cabinets and appliances are replaced. The median spend fora major kitchen remodel is $55,000.

Size makes a significant difference, especially for major projects. Renovating homeowners spend a median of $75,000 to remodel a larger kitchen (250 square feet or more), versus $46,000 for a smaller one (less than 250 square feet).

At the high end, spending climbs quickly. Among the top 10% of spenders, major remodels reach a median of $150,000 for larger kitchens and $120,000 for smaller ones.

Economists like to reference the median, or midpoint, figure rather than the average for this kind of data because the average can be skewed, can be volatile year over year and can be misleadingly high or low because of a single project that costs far more or less than others. The median spend reflects the reported costs of homeowners who completed a kitchen remodel by mid-2025 and is not adjusted for inflation.

[10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026](https://www.houzz.com/hznb/photos/10-kitchen-remodeling-trends-to-know-for-2026-phvw-vp~215066115 "10 Kitchen Remodeling Trends to Know for 2026") Save Photo

The median spend for minor kitchen updates, which involve replacing only some cabinets and appliances, is $20,000. Larger kitchens come in slightly higher at $25,000, compared with $20,000 for smaller spaces.

Among the top 10% of spenders, minor remodels reach a median spend of $50,000 overall — $53,000 for larger kitchens and $44,000 for smaller kitchens.

**8\. Pantry Cabinets Top Among Built-In Kitchen Features**

Built-ins continue to play a major role in kitchen remodels. More than three-quarters of homeowners (76%) include built-in features as part of their renovation.

Pantry cabinets top the list, added by nearly half of renovating homeowners (47%). Beverage stations rank second at 24%, reflecting the growing desire for dedicated drink and entertaining zones. Built-in seating such as banquettes and wine fridges are tied at 17%, followed by walk-in pantries (16%) and breakfast bars (14%). More specialized additions remain less common, with baking stations included by 9% of homeowners and butler’s pantries or prep kitchens by 7%.

**9\. Partial Cabinet Upgrade Strategies Shift**

Most kitchen remodels still involve a full cabinet overhaul. More than two-thirds of renovating homeowners (68%) replace all their cabinets, while just over a quarter (27%) opt for partial cabinet upgrades. Only 5% leave their cabinets untouched.

Among homeowners choosing a partial approach, refinishing cabinet exteriors remains the most common strategy (50%), though its share has slipped by 5 percentage points year over year. At the same time, adding some cabinets is gaining ground, rising 5 points to 32%. Replacing some cabinets (26%) and swapping only the doors (20%) continue to be popular options, while refinishing cabinet interiors has declined, down 5 points to 12%.

**10\. Most Homeowners Rely on Pros for Kitchen Remodels**

Kitchen renovations remain firmly in professional hands. The vast majority of renovating homeowners (87%) hire at least one pro for their kitchen project. [General contractors](https://www.houzz.com/professionals/general-contractor/) are the most commonly hired, engaged by nearly half of homeowners (48%), followed by building specialists — including electricians, plumbers and HVAC pros — at 42% and [cabinetmakers](https://www.houzz.com/professionals/cabinets/probr0-bo~t_11829) (34%). [Kitchen designers](https://www.houzz.com/professionals/hznb/kitchen-and-bath) are also key players, hired by more than 1 in 5 homeowners (22%), though that share is down 3 percentage points from the previous year. [Design-build firms](https://www.houzz.com/professionals/design-build/probr0-bo~t_11793) (13%), [interior designers](https://www.houzz.com/professionals/interior-designer/probr0-bo~t_11785) (12%), [architects](https://www.houzz.com/professionals/architect/probr0-bo~t_11784) (11%) and dedicated [kitchen remodelers](https://www.houzz.com/professionals/kitchen-and-bath-remodelers/probr0-bo~t_11825) (9%) round out the list.

Professional involvement is nearly universal on higher-end kitchen projects. Among renovating homeowners spending $50,000 or more, 98% hire at least one professional. General contractors lead the list (60%), followed by building specialists (53%) and cabinetmakers (43%).

Design expertise also plays a larger role at this price point. About one-third of homeowners (34%) work with a kitchen designer, while others turn to adesign-build firm (18%), an interior designer (13%), an architect (13%) or a dedicated kitchen remodeler (11%).

The 2026 U.S. Houzz Kitchen Trends Study gathered information from 1,780 Houzz users who reported being 18 years old or older, being a homeowner and having completed a kitchen remodel or addition in the past 12 months or to be currently working on one or planning to start one in the next six months. The survey was fielded between July 6 and July 30, 2025.

Makers of High End custom cabinets, furniture and custom millwork located in Brooklyn. Our master makers providing tailored solutions for your Home Furniture, Custom Kitchen and custom cabinet Design+Build and manufacturing needs servicing the New York City area. # Building custom home- how much should we spend on cabinets?

[shandrakg](https://www.houzz.com/user/shandrakg)

6 years ago

We are building a custom home on Lake Conroe in Texas. The overall cost is around 840K (not including what we paid for the property). Our builder gave us a 40k budget for cabinets. We have had 3 bids. The lowest coming in at 42K but we don't love those cabinets. The other 2 came in at 52K and 60K. What is the normal percentage to spend on cabinets for a custom home? The plan is for this to be a 20 year home, but I don't want to price ourselves out of resale market if for some reason we did not live there that long.

EmailSaveComment86Follow

Featured Answer

[![](https://st.hzcdn.com/fimgs/352344b4069a69c6_0200-w40-h40-b0-p0--.jpg)](https://www.houzz.com/user/tracy-simoneaux)

###### [Tracy Simoneaux](https://www.houzz.com/user/tracy-simoneaux)

5 years ago

I'm a Kitchen Designer, with 27 years experience. The rule of thumb should be, cabinets alone should run anywhere from 3.5 to 6% cost of the home. This does not include installation. That runs anywhere from 40 to 60 dollars a box. Every cut for crown fillers, base molding 5-15 dollars. Countertops run about 2-3% of a homes costs. Appliances average 2-7%. Total for cabinets, countertops, and appliances, 10-15%. This includes cabinets only in baths and laundry, not fixtures or appliances in those rooms. Hope that helps.

# 25 Home Design Trends Defining How We’ll Live in 2026

## See how accessible layouts, rich materials and wellness-focused spaces will shape homes this year

[![Mitchell Parker](https://st.hzcdn.com/fimgs/fca3b09f00e89059_5619-w40-h40-b0-p0--.jpg)](https://www.houzz.com/user/mitchell-parker)

[Mitchell Parker](https://www.houzz.com/user/mitchell-parker) January 1, 2026

Houzz Editorial Staff. Home design journalist writing about cool spaces, innovative trends, breaking news, industry analysis and humor.

More

SaveComment8Like63

Share

TwitterEmail

Print

In an increasingly fast-paced world, homeowners are rethinking home design with a focus on intention and ease. They’re prioritizing warmth, longevity and well-being, choosing spaces that feel personal, calming and built to last. Many of this year’s leading trends — rich materials, heritage-inspired details and wellness-focused features — reflect a desire for homes that support real life rather than chase novelty. Many of the spaces shown here were created by pros who use [Houzz Pro](https://www.houzz.com/hire-pro/why-hire-a-pro-using-houzz-pro?source=platform_ideabooks). Ready to get inspired? Explore these 25 home design trends expected to define 2026.

[Lil Shingle](https://www.houzz.com/photos/lil-shingle-contemporary-exterior-seattle-phvw-vp~214047701 "Lil Shingle")[Best Practice Architecture](https://www.houzz.com/user/imab) Save Photo

**1\. Smart Design for Aging in Place and Multigenerational Living**

Many homeowners are planning for aging in place by proactively addressing the needs of older household members. Houzz research shows that 66% address special needs during bathroom remodels, often incorporating curbless showers and grab bars. But accessibility is also influencing the entire home, with features such as single-level layouts, wider walkways and easy-reach kitchen storage like large drawers improving both safety and ease.

At the same time, the rise of multigenerational living is shaping layouts that balance independence with togetherness. ADUs (accessory dwelling units), connected outdoor spaces and clearly defined private and shared zones allow families to live comfortably under one roof while adapting to changing needs over time. Baby boomers — the largest cohort of renovating homeowners, according to [Houzz research](https://www.houzz.com/magazine/2025-u-s-houzz-and-home-renovation-trends-stsetivw-vs~181188659) — are driving this shift, accelerating the adoption of flexible design solutions that support aging in place and evolving family dynamics.

This Seattle home by [Best Practice Architecture](https://www.houzz.com/pro/imab/best-practice-architecture) blends aging-in-place design with multigenerational living. Single-level spaces, a ramp to the [front door](https://www.houzz.com/photos/lil-shingle-contemporary-entry-seattle-phvw-vp~214047702) and a main-floor primary suite make the home accessible, while a connected deck and lower-level ADU support gatherings and future family needs. Outdoor zones — including a zen garden and a fire pit lounge — extend living areas and highlight stunning lake and mountain views.

**2\. Traditional Details**

Traditional style is back, but not in the formal, buttoned-up way you might remember. Today’s interpretation feels warm, grounded and quietly elegant, drawing inspiration from English country and modern Tudor influences. In kitchens, inset cabinetry, plate racks, arched range hoods and richly stained woods bring a sense of craftsmanship and familiarity.

That shift is showing up in the data. The share of homeowners choosing traditional style rose 5 percentage pointscompared with the previous year in the [2025 U.S. Houzz Kitchen Trends Study](https://www.houzz.com/magazine/2025-u-s-houzz-kitchen-trends-study-stsetivw-vs~179685414), signaling renewed appreciation for comfort, classic detailing and enduring materials.

Throughout the home, curves, arches and scalloped edges soften spaces, while built-ins, paneling and detailed millwork add nuance and character. Earthy hues such as olive green, taupe and deep brown — paired with stained woods and muted blues or burgundies — reinforce the style’s everlasting charm.

This Seattle kitchen by [Jessica Nelson Design](https://www.houzz.com/pro/jessicaenelson/jessica-nelson-design) brings traditional details to life with an arched range niche, a wood island top and elegant stone surfaces. Classic millwork and heritage-inspired touches create a cozy, inviting space that feels timeless yet modern.

**3\. Warm and Medium Wood Tones**

Warm and medium wood tones are making a strong comeback, with rift- and quartersawn white oak, walnut, elm, hickory, cherry and cerused finishes replacing the once-dominant light oak. Darker stains are also resurging, bringing added depth, lushness and enduring elegance to kitchens, bathrooms, living rooms and bedrooms.

These woods are prized for versatility and warmth. In kitchens, walnut or cherry cabinetry paired with soft neutrals or subtly veined marble countertops creates a welcoming feel. In living areas and bedrooms, medium-toned flooring or wall paneling provides a cozy backdrop that harmonizes with deep greens, navy blues, muted terra cotta, cream and warm grays.

Set amid wetlands, steep slopes and mature trees near Washington’s Cougar Mountain, this kitchen by [H2D Architecture + Design](https://www.houzz.com/pro/h2darchitects/h2d-architecture-design) celebrates its wooded surroundings. Warm medium wood cabinets and flooring create a balanced, inviting feel that complements the home’s natural setting.

**4\. Warm, Earthy Colors Everywhere**

Terra cotta, sage, olive green, dusty blue, muted pastels, creamy beiges, browns, taupes and buttery yellows are taking over interiors. “The reign of all-white interiors and icy gray palettes has definitely come to an end,” says color specialist [Jennifer Ott](https://www.houzz.com/pro/jenott/jennifer-ott-design). “Homeowners are now craving warmth, richness and depth in their spaces. For those who still prefer lighter palettes, stark whites are giving way to warmer neutrals that are sun-warmed and tactile — think canvas, parchment or soft stone gray. These hues add subtle depth while maintaining a sense of calm and brightness.”

Kitchens feature terra-cotta-colored tile backsplashes and sage cabinetry, while living rooms lean into buttery yellows, warm taupes and olive accents layered with natural textures like linen, wool and rattan. Bedrooms and bathrooms are embracing muted blues and greens for a soothing, restorative feel, and even entryways and home offices are benefiting from warmer palettes that create inviting spaces rather than stark or clinical ones. “Clients have been increasingly drawn to warm, nature-inspired tones in their kitchen designs, particularly incorporating earthy hues like terra cotta, soft beige and sage green,” says designer [Donna Rose](https://www.houzz.com/pro/donnarose/donna-rose-design). “This trend aligns with the broader shift toward biophilic, nature-inspired design.”

This New Jersey living room by[Forina Design](https://www.houzz.com/pro/forinadesignandco/forina-design-co) showcases the warmth of woodsy tones. Like many of the pros featured in this story, Forina Design uses Houzz Pro. Moody green sofas, deep beige wallpaper, wood accents and touches of yellow, gold and blue create a layered, inviting space that feels both organic and vibrant.

**5\. Green as the New Neutral**

Green is stepping out of the accent shadows to become a defining hue in home design. Designers report strong demand for sage, olive, forest and deep green tones, especially in cabinetry, tile, upholstery and stone. Once used sparingly, green is now anchoring entire rooms, shaping the mood of kitchens, bathrooms and living spaces. Designers are often pairing green with handmade tile, deeply veined marble and warm wood tones, creating layered interiors that feel timeless, organic and connected to nature.

This Bellevue, Washington, kitchen by [Eastside Kitchen & Bath](https://www.houzz.com/pro/eastsidekitchen/eastside-kitchen-and-bath) shows how green can function as a neutral. Deep green cabinetry pairs with a creamy tile backsplash and island base for an effortlessly earthy look.

**6\. Warm Yellow Accents**

Earthy yellows like ocher, mustard and honey are reintroducing warmth and character to interiors. Designers are favoring these muted, grounded shades over bright citrus tones, using them in textiles, upholstery and accents to create a cozy, sun-washed glow. Paired with rust, burgundy and deep browns, warm yellow reads as understated and comforting rather than bold or flashy.

[Walden Interiors](https://www.houzz.com/pro/rishawalden/walden-interiors) brightened this New York midcentury-style living room with warm yellow accents. The muted golden tones add sunny warmth without overwhelming the space.

**7\. Integrated, Seamless and Hidden Features**

Open, multifunctional homes are inspiring smarter, more discreet design. Panel-ready appliances, concealed range hoods, pocket-door cabinets and hydraulic-lift storage units allow technology and essentials to recede into the architecture rather than compete with it.

Lighting and smart systems are often embedded directly into millwork and surfaces in the form of concealed cove lighting, illuminated niches, pop-up TVs and hidden charging stations. The result is a calm, uncluttered aesthetic that showcases craftsmanship and spatial flow.

This Chicago kitchen by [Lauren Collander Interiors](https://www.houzz.com/pro/danielle3744/lauren-collander-interiors) highlights integrated, hidden features that keep the space streamlined. Sliding backsplash slabs conceal storage. A paneled refrigerator and freezer and a butler’s pantry behind the range help keep the sightlines clear and the look clutter-free.

**8\. Flat-Panel Cabinets and Vanities**

Sleek and streamlined, flat-panel (or slab-front) cabinets and vanities are gaining ground, even in traditional- and transitional-style rooms. Smooth, unframed doors and subtle edge profiles give cabinets a custom look that feels modern yet refined. They’re also easy to wipe clean. Warm wood tones, matte finishes and minimalist hardware keep the style comfortable rather than stark. Flat-panel cabinetry ranks as the second-most popular style after Shaker, according to [Houzz research](https://www.houzz.com/magazine/2025-u-s-houzz-kitchen-trends-study-stsetivw-vs~179685414), showing that simplicity never loses its appeal.

**23\. Sustainability and Longevity**

Sustainability and longevity are guiding interior choices, with natural, durable and eco-conscious materials replacing short-lived, disposable pieces. Wood, stone, metal and high-quality textiles that age gracefully or can be repaired are complemented by vintage or secondhand furniture, resulting in character and a smaller environmental footprint.

The benefits are practical and aesthetic: Spaces feel grounded, elegant and thoughtfully curated, while materials last longer, reduce waste and support mindful consumption. This trend reflects a shift toward intentional living, where beauty, function and conscience coexist seamlessly throughout the home.

**24\. Immersive Visualization Becomes the New Blueprint**

More homeowners are looking for ways to picture their finished spaces before demo begins. With more than 1 in 5 saying they struggled to visualize the end result at the start of a project (22%), tools like 3D floor plans and AR walk-throughs are becoming essential for building confidence in design decisions. Platforms such as [Houzz Pro](https://www.houzz.com/hire-pro/why-hire-a-pro-using-houzz-pro), which allows pros to provide clients with realistic images, mood boards and curated product selections, make it easier for homeowners to explore possibilities and feel certain about their choices. **25.Transparent Renovation Projects**

In 2026 homeowners will look for more openness and clarity throughout their renovation journey. [Houzz research](https://blog.houzz.com/what-homeowners-want-during-home-renovation-projects/) shows that more than 40% of homeowners said a clearer timeline would have improved their project experience, and the same share wanted better communication from their pros. Expect digital project dashboards complete with easy-to-follow schedules, visual progress updates and payment timelines to become a regular part of how construction and design firms keep homeowners informed.

## About Us

At Upstate Millwork, we are dedicated to fostering long-term partnerships with our clients across the Carolinas and Georgia. Specializing in architectural millwork, we provide a comprehensive range of services, including plastic laminate cabinets, custom assembly, high-end woodworking, and solid surface fabrication. Our skilled project management and installation teams ensure that every project is delivered on time and to the highest standards.

We serve a variety of sectors, including healthcare, education, industrial, and municipal, offering tailored solutions for office upfits, veterinary spaces, and more. Through clear communication and actionable processes, we bring projects to life with precision, craftsmanship, and reliability.
